questions.question: 中文导读：A YOLO detector with grid 13x13, 3 anchors per cell, and 20 classes produces a head output of shape (N, 13, 13, 3, 25). Why is the last dimension 25?
options: 中文选项：4 box coords + 1 bias + 20 classes
中文选项：4 box coords + 1 objectness + 20 classes = 5 + C
中文选项：25 is the number of classes in COCO
中文选项：It is padding to the next power of 2
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Every anchor emits four box regression targets (tx, ty, tw, th), one objectness score, and C class logits. For YOLO-style detectors the per-anchor dimension is always 5 + C. On COCO with 80 classes it is 85; on PASCAL VOC with 20 it is 25.

questions.question: 中文导读：Why does YOLO predict tx, ty through a sigmoid before adding the grid cell?
options: 中文选项：To normalise them to [0, 1] so the centre stays inside the cell and cannot escape to a neighbouring cell's territory
中文选项：Sigmoid is required by the convolution
中文选项：To prevent NaN during training
中文选项：To make the box larger
questions.explanation: 中文解析：sigmoid(tx) is in [0, 1], so (sigmoid(tx) + cell_x) * stride places the centre somewhere inside the cell at column cell_x. This constraint keeps each cell responsible for only the objects centred within its own area, which is the assumption that makes grid-based training stable.

questions.question: 中文导读：Your detector predicts 15 boxes around a single object after the head. You apply NMS with iou_threshold=0.45 and get back 1 box. What did NMS do?
options: 中文选项：It averaged all 15 predictions
中文选项：It sorted the 15 boxes by score, kept the highest-scoring, and deleted every box whose IoU with it exceeded 0.45; the other 14 all overlapped the winner above the threshold so one remained
中文选项：It picked a random survivor
中文选项：It merged the boxes geometrically
questions.explanation: 中文解析：NMS is a greedy deduplication: sort by score, keep the top, remove everything close to it (IoU > threshold), repeat. It does not merge or average. That is why detection latency benchmarks include NMS time and why modern models like RT-DETR replace it with a learned alternative.

questions.question: 中文导读：Your model has mAP@0.5 = 0.75 but mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.32. What is the most accurate interpretation?
options: 中文选项：The model is broken; mAP@0.5 must equal mAP@0.5:0.95
中文选项：The model finds the right objects but its boxes are not tightly localised — fine at IoU 0.5, fail at IoU 0.7+
中文选项：The dataset has too many classes
中文选项：The inference threshold is too high
questions.explanation: 中文解析：mAP@0.5 is lenient about localisation; mAP@0.5:0.95 averages over strict thresholds up to 0.95. A big gap between the two means the boxes are in roughly the right place but not tight. Typical fixes: CIoU/DIoU box loss, higher-resolution features, anchor sets better tuned to the dataset.

questions.question: 中文导读：YOLO loss weights lambda_coord=5.0 and lambda_noobj=0.5 roughly mirror the original paper. What do these ratios encode?
options: 中文选项：GPU memory trade-offs

中文选项：The fact that most cells have no object, so the no-object cells must be downweighted or they would dominate the total loss; and the fact that box regression is a smaller-scale loss than cross-entropy, so it needs upweighting to produce comparable gradients
中文选项：Random choices the authors never justified
中文选项：The number of anchors per cell
questions.explanation: 中文解析：In a 13x13 grid there are 169 cells, usually with one to five objects. The 160+ empty cells all contribute objectness loss; without a small lambda_noobj they would overwhelm the positive signal. Conversely, MSE box loss is numerically small next to cross-entropy, so lambda_coord > 1 keeps its gradients comparable. Both ratios are about balancing per-component gradient magnitudes.
